title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that help pedestrians understand the vehicle's intended behavior at crosswalks, improve their receptivity toward FAVs, and affect their crossing behavior. The study addresses the communication gap between FAVs and pedestrians since interpersonal interaction is not possible with driverless vehicles.
method_summary: Virtual reality experiment using Unity 3D software with HTC Vive headset. Thirty participants walked across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Four visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' in text, flashing smile, no signal) and four audible features (horn sound, music, verbal message 'safe to cross', no signal) were tested in a 4x4 randomized complete block design. Data collected through simulator (crossing time, waiting time, head tracking) and surveys (baseline receptivity, demographic questionnaire, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale, after-study receptivity).
tasks: Pedestrian crossing behavior at unsignalized crosswalk in presence of FAV with external communication features
metrics: Feature ratings (1-7 Likert scale), crossing time (seconds), waiting time before crossing (seconds), pedestrian behavior questionnaire (PBQ) scores, pedestrian receptivity questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF) scores, personal innovativeness scores
claim_text: External features on FAVs significantly increased pedestrians' receptivity toward fully autonomous vehicles
limitations: Single controlled scenario at unsignalized intersection with FAV approaching from one direction and always stopping | No other traffic present in virtual environment to eliminate distractions and ensure focus on features | Features indicated only safe crossing conditions; vehicle stopped in every trial; did not test feature termination or vehicle restart signals | First-time exposure to FAV; results may differ with familiarity over time | Limited sample size of 30 participants; findings may not generalize to broader population | US-focused study with English-speaking participants; cross-cultural differences in feature preferences not captured
notes: VR validation study (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017) confirmed participants responded similarly in VR as in real traffic. Walking speed in simulator (1.54 m/s) was faster than typical pedestrian speed (1.20 m/s), possibly due to discomfort with driverless vehicle. Study received IRB approval from Mississippi State University.

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Understanding what factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, with the aim of proposing countermeasures to reduce pedestrian-related traffic crashes
method_summary: A binomial logistic regression model was established using SP (stated preference) survey data collected from 631 valid pedestrian samples in Hefei, China. The survey captured pedestrian socio-economic characteristics, trip-related features, and attitude variables. Score test was used for variable selection at 95% confidence level, followed by iterative calculation to identify significant predictors of red-light running behavior.
tasks: analyze factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior; predict probability of red-light running based on significant variables
datasets: 631 valid pedestrian survey samples from Hefei, China (collected May-June 2015)
metrics: Score test significance (p<0.05); odds ratios; model coefficients; confidence intervals
claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running behavior: trip purpose (stroll vs work/school), time period in a day (daytime vs dawn), attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether quality of road facility affects crossing behavior
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei) | The study only considered signalized intersections, not other crossing facility types (e.g., midblock crossings) | Study focused on individual pedestrian behavior, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons/groups | Results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences
notes: This is a behavioral/traffic safety study, not an ML paper. The methodology uses traditional binomial logistic regression rather than machine learning. Key contribution is identifying that pedestrian attitudes (not just socio-economic characteristics) significantly affect red-light running behavior in Chinese context.

Key contribution is identifying that pedestrian attitudes (not just socio-economic characteristics) significantly affect red-light running behavior in Chinese context.

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Understanding what factors affect pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at signalized intersections in China, with the aim of proposing countermeasures to reduce pedestrian-related traffic crashes
method_summary: A binomial logistic regression model was established using SP (stated preference) survey data collected from 631 valid pedestrian samples in Hefei, China. metrics: Score test significance (p<0.05); odds ratios; model coefficients; confidence intervals
claim_text: Pedestrians who believe road facility quality affects their crossing behavior are more likely to run red-lights (odds ratio 0.483 for those unaffected)
limitations: Data were collected in only one Chinese city (Hefei) | The study only considered signalized intersections, not other crossing facility types (e.g., midblock crossings) | Study focused on individual pedestrian behavior, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons/groups | Results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences

Key contribution is identifying that pedestrian attitudes (not just socio-economic characteristics) significantly affect red-light running behavior in Chinese context.

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: This study examines both personal factors (pedestrian demographics and behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition) affecting the individual decision of red light running violation at signalized urban intersections in Hong Kong.
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong, capturing crossing behaviors of 6,320 pedestrians during 652 signal cycles. A random parameter logit model was developed to measure the association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity in pedestrian behavior.
datasets: 6,320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles; Urban crosswalk locations: Prince Edward Road West j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Sai Yee Street, Tonkin Street j/w Shun Ning Road, Hung Hom South Road j/w Po loi Street, To Kwa Wan Road j/w Chi Kiang Street
claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667), with 64.3% probability that females have lower propensity than males
limitations: Study focuses on propensity of red light running but does not assess safety outcomes (associated crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior was not measured | Data on pedestrian safety perception and attitude toward traffic rules was not collected, limiting understanding of decision-making trade-offs | Observation survey conducted only on weekdays during daytime, may not capture夜间或周末行为差异
notes: Study addresses gap in literature by examining both individual-level (personal demographics and behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition and signal time) factors together; Novel contribution includes examining social norm effects through presence and behavior of other pedestrians; Random parameter approach justified by significant standard deviation of gender effect, indicating unobserved heterogeneity in risk-taking attitude among female pedestrians; 18.5% of observed pedestrians (1,169 out of 6,320) committed red light running violations; Hong Kong pedestrian signals have three phases: steady green, flashing green, and steady red - no countdown display present

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: This study examines both personal factors (pedestrian demographics and behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition) affecting the individual decision of red light running violation at signalized urban intersections in Hong Kong.

limitations: Study focuses on propensity of red light running but does not assess safety outcomes (associated crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior was not measured | Data on pedestrian safety perception and attitude toward traffic rules was not collected, limiting understanding of decision-making trade-offs | Observation survey conducted only on weekdays during daytime, may not capture夜间或周末行为差异
notes: Study addresses gap in literature by examining both individual-level (personal demographics and behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition and signal time) factors together; Novel contribution includes examining social norm effects through presence and behavior of other pedestrians; Random parameter approach justified by significant standard deviation of gender effect, indicating unobserved heterogeneity in risk-taking attitude among female pedestrians; 18.5% of observed pedestrians (1,169 out of 6,320) committed red light running violations; Hong Kong pedestrian signals have three phases: steady green, flashing green, and steady red - no countdown display present
